context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I need help with a 700–800 word reflection on migration and development rn. i’m kinda confused how to connect remittances, the state’s role, and the scalar/transnational stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Hey, welcome! Good that you're tackling this early. Before we dig into structure, let's start with what you already know. In your own words, what do you think the connection between migration and development is? Don't overthink it — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced themselves and the assignment. I should welcome them and use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the core concepts before diving into structure. I'll start with a foundational question to assess what they already know rather than scaffolding everything upfront.

exchanges.student: i think migration affects development bc people move for jobs and then send money back home, but that feels too basic lol. maybe it also changes local labor, families, and even politics?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid starting point — you're already thinking beyond just the money flow. So let's push on that. The prompt asks whether remittances are the 'new development mantra.' What do you think that phrase means, and do you think it's a good thing or a problematic label?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good intuitive grasp of the basics and is already self-critical, which is promising. I should validate the direction without confirming it as complete, and push them to think more critically about remittances specifically since that's a key part of the prompt. I'll ask a focused Socratic question about remittances to move toward the 'new development mantra' angle.

exchanges.student: ngl I read that as like people overhyping remittances as the solution to development. so maybe it’s helpful, but kinda problematic if it ignores inequality or lets states do less?
exchanges.tutor: That's a really insightful tension you've identified. So if remittances can let states off the hook, what do you think states *should* be doing instead — or in addition — to make migration actually work for development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sharp critical observation about remittances potentially letting states off the hook. This is a key tension in the literature and directly relevant to the next part of the prompt about the state's role. I should affirm the direction and use it as a bridge to ask about what the state *should* do, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: i’m not totally sure, but maybe states should make migration safer and cheaper, and not just depend on people leaving? like policies for labor rights, banking access, and actual domestic development too?
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking — you're already distinguishing between different levels of state action. That actually connects nicely to the 'scalar approach' the prompt mentions. When you think about migration, what different *scales* or levels do you think are involved — and why might that matter for understanding its development impacts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent argument about the state's role. They've identified concrete policy areas. I should affirm this and now pivot to the scalar/transnational concepts, which they flagged as confusing at the start. A Socratic question linking their state-level thinking to the scalar approach would move the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: idk if I’m getting “scalar” right, but maybe it means local, national, and global levels? like a migrant helps a household first, but then there are city, state, and international effects too.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right idea on scalar. Now, the prompt also asks about a 'transnational optic.' How do you think that's different from just looking at those multiple scales? What does 'transnational' add to our understanding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly intuited the scalar concept. I should confirm the direction and now bridge to the transnational optic, which is the last major concept they need to address. A focused question connecting scalar thinking to the transnational lens will help them synthesize.

exchanges.student: this is the part messing me up tbh. is transnational basically just cross-border stuff, or is it more like migrants living connected to 2 places at once?
exchanges.tutor: Your second instinct is much stronger — sit with that idea. What does it mean practically for development if a migrant is *simultaneously* connected to two places, rather than just moving from one to another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling productively with an important distinction. Their second instinct — migrants living connected to two places at once — is much closer to the mark and I should push them to develop it further rather than just confirming it. This will help them articulate the concept in their own words for the paper.

exchanges.student: i guess development wouldn’t be a one-country story then. migrants can send money, ideas, skills, and political influence back home while also shaping the place they moved to.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong synthesis — you've actually touched on remittances, ideas/skills (sometimes called 'social remittances'), and political influence. You now have the building blocks for all five parts of the prompt. Have you thought about how you'd organize all of this into a coherent 700-800 word piece? What might your structure look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the transnational optic well — covering remittances, social remittances, and political transnationalism. This is a strong synthesis. We've now covered all the major concepts in the prompt. Given we're at exchange 7 and have ~3 left, I should push the student to start integrating all of this into a written attempt, since they have enough material to work with.

exchanges.student: not really lol, i have the ideas but not the order. can u help me make a simple structure that answers all parts without sounding like a list?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job, not mine! But think about it this way: looking back at our conversation, which ideas naturally *led into* other ideas? That flow might already suggest an order. Give it a shot — what would you put first and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure their paper for them, which would cross into doing the work for them. I should redirect and ask them to propose a structure themselves based on what we've discussed, using a Socratic question. I can hint that the concepts we discussed have natural connections that could guide the flow.

exchanges.student: maybe first I define migration-development broadly, then argue remittances matter but are overrated, then bring in the state, and after that use scalar + transnational ideas to show the full picture? i’m not sure if that flows tho.
exchanges.tutor: That flow actually makes good sense — you move from broad context to critique to solutions to analytical frameworks. It builds an argument rather than just listing. You have all the ideas. Now it's time to write a draft — stop planning and start writing! Come back when you have something on paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has proposed a solid structure that covers all parts of the prompt in a logical order. The flow is actually quite good — it moves from broad to specific to analytical frameworks. I should affirm the logic briefly and push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan, since we're at exchange 9 and they have everything they need.

exchanges.student: ok yeah that helps, thanks. before i draft, can u just tell me if that sounds like a clear thesis direction or if it’s still too vague?
exchanges.tutor: You haven't actually told me your thesis yet — just your structure. What is the *central argument* you want your reader to walk away with? Try to say it in one sentence, then go write!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for validation before writing, which is a slight stall tactic. I haven't heard an actual thesis statement yet — just a structure. I should redirect them to articulate their actual thesis/argument in one sentence, which will force them to commit to a position and then start writing. This is the last exchange so I should wrap up productively.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. Every key idea — the critique of remittances as a 'mantra,' the state's role, scalar levels, and the transnational optic — was drawn out from the student through targeted questions rather than being stated by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on the student's prior responses, validated partial understanding, and redirected toward deeper thinking. Responses were concise and well-calibrated to the student's level. No submission-ready content was provided, no editing was done, and the student was pushed to articulate their own thesis at the end.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1: the tutor never gave a final answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key interpretive conclusions before the student reached them. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged student progress (e.g., 'That's a really insightful tension,' 'You've got the right idea on scalar') and built each question on the student's prior response rather than restarting. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback when appropriate, such as noting the student had 'building blocks for all five parts' and distinguishing structure from thesis in Turn 10. Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation advanced progressively without repetition, stayed anchored to the assignment, and the tutor redirected the student's request for a pre-made structure back to the student in Turn 8. Communication was consistently bite-sized, warm, and non-evaluative in tone.
